pages.text: Mechanical properties of kenaf ﬁber reinforced concrete A. Elsaid a,⇑, M. Dawood b, R. Seracino a, C. Bobko a a Department of Civil, Construction, and Environmental Engineering, North Carolina State University, NC 27965-7533, United States b Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Huston, TX 77204-4003, United States article info Article history: Received 25 February 2010 Received in revised form 22 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 10 December 2010 Keywords: Sustainable construction Toughness Scanning electron microscopy Compressive strength Compressive modulus Splitting tension strength Flexural behavior abstract This paper presents the ﬁndings of an experimental research program that was conducted to study the mechanical properties of a natural ﬁber reinforced concrete (FRC) which is made using the bast ﬁbers of the kenaf plant. The kenaf plant is quickly developing as a replacement crop for the dwindling tobacco industry in the south-eastern United States. Appropriate mixture proportions and mixing procedures are recommended to produce kenaf FRC (KFRC) with ﬁber volume contents of 1.2% and 2.4%. The compressive strength, compressive modulus, splitting tensile strength and modulus of rupture of KFRC specimens are presented and compared to the properties of plain concrete control specimens. The experimental results indicate that the mechanical properties of KFRC are comparable to those of plain concrete control spec- imens, particularly when accounting for the effect of the increased w/c ratio required to produce work- able KFRC. Further, the results indicate that KFRC generally exhibits more distributed cracking and higher toughness than plain concrete. Scanning electron micrographs (SEM’s) indicate that a good bond between the kenaf ﬁbers and the surrounding matrix is achieved. The SEM’s also provide interesting information regarding the mechanisms which contribute to the failure and post-peak behavior of the KFRC which may be beneﬁcial to future modeling efforts. The research ﬁndings indicate that KFRC is a promising ‘green’ construction material which could potentially be used in a number of different structural applications. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The advantages of natural ﬁber reinforced concrete (FRC), includ- ing increased toughness, enhanced cracking behavior, enhanced durability and improved fatigue and impact resistance have been well documented previously [1,2]. Recently, a crop known as kenaf has been studied as a potential replacement for the diminishing to- bacco farming industry in the south-eastern United States. While several other types of natural ﬁbers have been studied for produc- tion of FRC, investigations on the use of kenaf have been limited. A recent study indicates that the kenaf plant absorbs approximately 1.5 times its weight in carbon dioxide, which represents the highest level of absorption of all of the plants studied [3]. The use of high volumes of kenaf ﬁbers to produce kenaf FRC (KFRC) would offset a considerable amount of the carbon dioxide released to the atmo- sphere during the production of Portland cement thereby making it a more sustainable, ‘green’ material. This application represents an intriguing method of sequestering carbon by using the kenaf plant to absorb CO 2 from the atmosphere subsequently ‘locking’ it into concrete structures. This paper presents a summary of the ﬁnd- ings of an exploratory study t

of the kenaf plant is made up of a complex porous structure with a primary pore structure and minor pores which form an intercon- nected pore structure. The density of the bast ﬁber is 1.293 ± 0.006 gm/cm 3 [6] while the density of the core ﬁber is 0.09–0.11 gm/cm3 [7]. In addition to the micro-pores in its struc- ture the material consists of approximately 50% by weight of cellu- lose which renders it highly absorbent [5]. The Naval Facilities Engineering Service Center has evaluated kenaf core as an absor- bent material for cleaning oil spills. The research showed that the core particles outperformed other natural absorbent materials by a signiﬁcant margin [7]. Several studies have been conducted to characterize the basic tensile properties of kenaf bast ﬁbers [8–11]. The results indicate that the average tensile strength of kenaf ﬁbers range from 157 MPa to 600 MPa. The average ultimate tensile strain and elas- tic modulus of the ﬁbers range from 0.015 to 0.019 and 12,800 MPa to 34,200 MPa respectively. The mechanical properties of the ﬁbers depend on the location along the stalk from which the ﬁbers are ta- ken [9]. Fibers from the middle of the stalk tend to be stronger and stiffer while ﬁbers taken from the ends of the stalk tend to be weaker and more ﬂexible possibly due to exposure to environmen- tal conditions, decay, and damage caused by insects and other pests. The study further indicated that the stress–strain curve of the kenaf ﬁbers is approximately linear to failure regardless of the location from which the ﬁbers are taken. The large variability of the reported ﬁber properties are due to the inherent variability of natural materials and also possibly due to the wide variety of sub-species that are commonly tested under the ‘kenaf’ name [12]. Inspection indicates that the tensile properties of kenaf ﬁbers are comparable to those of other natural ﬁbers, such as jute, ﬂax and bamboo, which have been previously used to produce natural ﬁber reinforced concrete [13]. A detailed discussion of the material properties of kenaf ﬁbers is beyond the scope of this paper. Addi- tional details are reported in Elsaid, et al. [4]. 2.2. Natural ﬁber reinforced concrete The use of natural FRC has been studied quite heavily over the past 40 years. The properties of natural FRC are dependent on a number of factors including the type and the length of ﬁbers used and the volume fraction. Early research indicated that the mini- mum ﬁber volume fraction required to provide signiﬁcant improvement in the mechanical properties of cement composites was approximately 3% [14]. In this study the impact resistance was improved regardless of the ﬁber content, but other properties did not improve signiﬁcantly at lower ﬁber contents. Studies of jute FRC indicate that in general, compressive strength is not sig- niﬁcantly affected by the addition of ﬁbers, while tensile and ﬂex- ural strength and toughness are all substantially increased [15]. Applications of natural FRC for large-scale structural purposes have traditionally been limited to special applications which are practically and economically justiﬁed. One of the most promising ﬁelds for their application is that of composite construction in which the natural FRC forms a permanent strong and tough cover- ing over a weaker core. Natural FRC further provides architectural and ornamental features [16–18]. Sisal ﬁber reinforced concrete tiles, corrugated rooﬁng sheets, pipes, gas tanks, water tanks and si

5.1. Compressive strength tests A total of 48–100 mm diameter /C2 200 mm long concrete cylinders were tested according to ASTM Standard C 39 [21] to evaluate the compressive strength charac- teristics of KFRC as compared to plain concrete. The cylinders were capped with neoprene caps and tested in a 2000 kN Forney hydraulic testing machine. The load was applied at a constant loading rate to induce a constant rate of stress in the cyl- inder of 241 ± 48 kPa/s. The peak failure load and the failure mode were recorded. 5.2. Compressive elastic modulus tests A total of 22–100 mm diameter /C2 200 mm long concrete cylinders were tested to evaluate the compressive elastic modulus of KFRC as compared to plain concrete. The ends of the cylinders were cut using a wet-cut concrete saw to eliminate the need for using neoprene caps. The relative movement between the platens of the testing machine was measured using three opposing pairs of electrical resistance potentiometers as shown in Fig. 3 . Each pair of potentiometers was attached to a steel channel to ensure that they remained properly aligned and protected through- out the test. The potentiometer pairs were placed around the cylinder at 120 /C176inter- vals, equidistant from the surface of the cylinder as shown schematically in Fig. 3 . The cylinders were loaded to approximately 40% of the measured compressive strength and unloaded three times and ﬁnally loaded to failure following the load- ing sequence prescribed by ASTM C 469 [22]. The load was applied at a constant loading rate to induce a constant stress rate in the cylinders of 241 ± 34 kPa/s. The applied load and the deﬂection of the potentiometers were continuously re- corded using an Optim Electronics Megadac data acquisition system. The concrete strain was calculated by dividing the average measured platen displacement by the measured cylinder length. The applied stress was calculated by dividing the ap- plied load by the cross-sectional area of the cylinder. The chord elastic modulus of the concrete, E, was calculated as: E ¼ r2 /C0 r1 e2 /C0 0:00005 ð1Þ where r2 and e2 are the stress and strain at 40% of the ultimate strength of the con- crete and r1 is the stress corresponding to a strain of 0.00005. 5.3. Splitting tension tests The 28 day splitting tensile strength of the KFRC was measured and compared to that of the plain concrete control cylinders. The splitting tensile strength was ta- ken as an indication of the direct tension strength of the concrete and was evaluated primarily for comparison purposes. A total of 12–100 mm diameter /C2 200 mm long concrete cylinders were tested according to ASTM C 496 [23]. 5.4. Flexural tests The ﬂexural strength and toughness of KFRC prisms were evaluated according to ASTM C 1609 [24]. A total of 19–150 mm /C2 150 mm /C2 500 mm concrete prisms were tested in four-point bending with a span, L, of 450 mm. The load was applied at the 1/3 points using a 980 kN MTS hydraulic testing machine as shown in Fig. 4. The deﬂection of the beam was measured using linear electrical resistance potenti- ometers at a total of four locations: two at midspan and one at each support as shown schematically in Fig. 4 . The net midspan deﬂection, Dnet was calculated as: Dnet ¼ Dmid þ Dstroke /C0 Dsupport ð2Þ where Dmid is the average of the measured midspan deﬂections (measured relative to the actuator head), Dstroke is the measured stroke of the actuator (measured auto- matically), and Dsupport is the average o

within the context of previous research ﬁndings and relevant code provisions. 6.1. Compressive strength tests A series of compression tests were conducted to evaluate the early age strength of KFRC at 23 h and the 28 day strength. The cylinder behavior was compared to that of plain concrete control cylinders. The tested control cylinders typically exhibited very lit- tle cracking prior to failure. Failure typically occurred suddenly in a brittle manner by spalling of the concrete near the edges of the cyl- inder at one end as shown in Fig. 5 . In contrast, the tested KFRC typically exhibited a more ductile failure mode with well distrib- uted cracks which formed progressively prior to failure. The cylin- ders typically exhibited a columnar type failure characterized by the formation of large vertical cracks. In some cases this was accompanied by the formation of a diagonal shear crack near the mid-height of the cylinder as shown in Fig. 5 . This trend was ob- served consistently regardless of the age of the specimens at the time of testing. Inspection of the failed cylinders indicated a good distribution of the ﬁbers throughout the concrete as shown in Fig. 6 indicating that the mixing procedure was effective. The average measured compressive strengths of the tested cyl- inders at 23 h and 28 days are given in Tables 3 and 4 respectively. The tables give the mean, standard deviation and coefﬁcient of variation of the measured compressive strength for each group of cylinders. Inspection of the tables indicates that the addition of the kenaf ﬁbers to the concrete mixture reduced the average measured compressive strength of the concrete. Fig. 7a and b dem- onstrate the effect of the ﬁber volume fraction on the measured To data acquisition Bearing blocks Concrete cylinder Potentiometers Steel channel Steel channel Concrete cylinder Potentiometer location Section Side view Fig. 3. Elsaid et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1991–2001 1995

compressive strength of the cylinders tested after 23 h and 28 days respectively. The ﬁgure presents the measured concrete strength, fc, for all of the tested cylinders normalized with respect to the mean strength of the plain concrete control cylinders, fc,0% of the corresponding batches of concrete. The ﬁgure indicates a trend of decreasing compressive strength with increasing ﬁber content. From the slope of the plotted trend line, it can be seen that the 23 h and 28 day compressive strengths decrease by approximately 19% and 15% respectively for every 1% increase of ﬁber content. The observed reduction of strength is likely due to several fac- tors. As noted previously, in order to maintain the workability of the KFRC at an acceptable level, additional water was added to the concrete mixture while adding the ﬁbers. The w/c ratios of the KFRC with ﬁber contents of 0%, 1.2% and 2.4% were 0.35, 0.39 and 0.42 respectively. Fig. 8 illustrates the effect of the w/c ratio on the 28 day compressive strength for typical non-air entrained plain concrete [25]. The ﬁgure also superimposes the average mea- sured compressive strength of the KFRC cylinders with 0%, 1.2% and 2.4% ﬁber contents at their corresponding w/c ratios. Inspec- tion of the ﬁgure indicates that the measured compressive strengths of the cylinders with ﬁber contents of 0% and 1.2% are consistent with the predicted strength of non-air-entrained, plain concrete cylinders with similar w/c ratios. This indicates that the measured reduction of the compressive strength is due primarily to the additional water needed to enhance the workability of the mixture. Thus, the addition of the kenaf ﬁbers up to a ﬁber volume fraction of 1.2% had essentially no effect on the measured 28 day compressive strength. Inspection further indicates that the strength of the KFRC cylinders with a ﬁber content of 2.4% is less than the predicted strength of non-air-entrained, plain concrete cylinders with a similar w/c ratio. Thus, it appears that for higher ﬁber contents, the measured reduction of compression strength is due not only to the increase of the w/c ratio necessary for work- ability, but also due to the presence of high volume fraction of ke- naf ﬁbers in the concrete. 6.2. Compressive elastic modulus tests A total of 22 concrete cylinders were tested to evaluate the compressive stress–strain behavior of KFRC compared to plain con- crete control cylinders. The measured 28 day stress–strain rela- tionships of the KFRC and control cylinders are shown in Fig. 9 . The KFRC cylinders had a target ﬁber content of 1.2%. The trend of the stress–strain curves was similar for all of the tested cylinders with the two different ﬁber contents and for the tests after 23 h and 28 days. For comparison purposes the typical stress–strain curve for a typical portland cement mortar after 28 days is also given in the ﬁgure [26]. Inspection of the ﬁgure indicates that the KFRC cylinders typically exhibit lower compressive strength and modulus but higher toughness than the plain concrete control cylinders. The ﬁgure also indicates that the measured strain at peak stress, e0 c for the tested cylinders was within the range of 0.004–0.005. This is higher than the typical value of 0.002 expected for conventional concrete but lower than the value of 0.01 which is typical for mortars. This is primarily due to the small diameter and Fig. 5. Typical failure modes for (a) plain concrete control cylinder and (b) KFRC cylinder.

the relatively small quantity of coarse aggregates used in the mix- tures compared to conventional concrete. The failure mode of the tested cylinders was similar to that of the compressive strength cylinders with the KFRC cylinders typically exhibiting more well distributed cracking. The measured elastic modulus of the tested KFRC and plain con- crete control cylinders are given in Tables 5 and 6 . The tables give the mean, standard deviation and coefﬁcient of variation of the measured compressive strength and elastic modulus of the differ- ent groups of cylinders after 23 h and 28 days respectively. Inspec- tion of the tables indicates that the increase of the elastic modulus with increased curing time progressed as expected. The tables also indicate that the presence of the kenaf ﬁbers decreased the elastic modulus of the concrete, although a trend with increasing ﬁber content is not apparent. It should be noted that the increased porosity from the mixing process and the higher w/c ratio than the control might also decrease the compressive modulus of elasticity. The tables also give the normalized elastic modulus, Ec/ ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ fc p ; to account for the variability of the concrete strength due to the var- iation of the w/c. To facilitate comparison with the ACI 318M-08 [27] expression 57,000 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ fc p (psi), the normalized elastic modulus is given in both SI metric and US Customary units. Comparison of (b)(a) Fig. 7. Effect of ﬁber content on compressive strength of KFRC at (a) 23 h (b) 28 days. 0% FVF 1.2% FVF 2.4% FVF Fig. 8. Effect of w/c ratio on 28 day strength of plain concrete (adapted from ACI 211.1 [25]). KFRC (1.2%) Control Typical mortar Fig. 9. Measured stress–strain curve for KFRC and plain concrete control cylinders at 28 days. A. Elsaid et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1991–2001 1997

the normalized elastic modulus with the value of 57,000 provided by ACI 318-08 indicates that the modulus of the KFRC and the tested control cylinders is approximately 20% to 30% of the value recommended by ACI for a similar concrete strength. This reduc- tion of modulus is primarily due to the small diameter and the rel- atively small quantity of coarse aggregates used in the KFRC mixtures compared to conventional concrete. The dramatic reduc- tion of the elastic modulus of the KFRC could lead to a signiﬁcant increase of deﬂections which should be carefully considered in de- sign, particularly for structures in which the serviceability limit state is critical. However, for applications in which deﬂections are not critical, such as for slabs-on-grade, the advantages of the increased toughness and more distributed cracking of the KFRC will outweigh the disadvantage of the decreased modulus. 6.3. Splitting tension tests The 28 day splitting tension strength of the two KFRC mixtures was evaluated and compared to the behavior of corresponding plain concrete control specimens. The control cylinders exhibited a brittle failure due to the formation of a single splitting crack along the vertical diameter of the cylinder. By comparison, the KFRC cylinders exhibited a more ductile failure mode and a more distributed cracking pattern with clearly visible kenaf ﬁbers bridg- ing across the cracks. It was further observed that for the KFRC cylinders the ﬁrst cracking load was lower than the peak load indi- cating the beneﬁcial contribution of the kenaf ﬁbers to the tensile strength of the concrete. The ﬁrst cracking load and tensile split- ting load for the tested cylinders are given in Table 7 . Inspection of the table indicates that the KFRC cylinders exhibited a higher splitting tension strength and slightly lower variability of the ten- sion strength than the plain concrete cylinders. The table also indi- cates that, when normalized to account for the variation of the compressive strength, the tensile splitting strength is proportional to the ﬁber volume content of the concrete. Inspection of the table further indicates that the average measured splitting tension strength of the control cylinders is lower than the approximate va- lue of 0.56 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c p (MPa) [from 6.7 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c p (psi)] recommended by ACI 318-08 [27] for normal weight concrete while the average mea- sured strength of the KFRC cylinders is higher than the ACI approx- imation. This suggests that the ACI 318-08 approximation is conservative for KFRC. It should be mentioned that tensile proper- ties of concrete are highly variable. The small number of tests that were conducted was not intended to provide a complete statistical assessment of the tested values relative to the code recommenda- tions. They were only intended to give a meaningful basis for com- parison between KFRC and plain concrete. 6.4. Flexural tests Flexural tests were conducted to evaluate the effect of the kenaf ﬁbers on the ﬂexural strength and toughness of KFRC prisms. The measured load-midspan deﬂection behavior of the tested 28 days control prisms and the 1.2% KFRC prisms are given in Fig. 10 a and b respectively. Inspection indicates that the presence of the kenaf ﬁbers slightly decreased the peak ﬂexural strength of the concrete but signiﬁcantly increased the residual ﬂexural strength and toughness of the prisms which is typical for FRC. The typical failure mode of the tested control and KFRC prisms are

7. Conclusions This paper presents the ﬁndings of an experimental research program that was conducted to evaluate the basic characteristics of kenaf ﬁber reinforced concrete (KFRC). Suitable mixture propor- tions were established for KFRC with ﬁber contents of 1.2% and 2.4%. The research indicates that in order to maintain the workabil- ity of the KFRC requires the use of a cement rich mixture and coarse aggregates with a maximum diameter of 9.5 mm. Further, additional water should be added to account for water absorbed by the ﬁbers and to maintain the workability of the fresh KFRC. The ﬁndings of the experimental program indicate that at lower ﬁber contents, the strength of KFRC is similar to that of non-air- entrained, plain concrete with a similar w/c ratio. At higher ﬁber contents, the compressive strength of KFRC is somewhat lower than that of non-air-entrained plain concrete with a similar w/c ra- tio. In general KFRC specimens exhibited more ductile behavior with greater energy absorption and more well distributed cracking patterns which is typical for FRC. The research further indicates that due to the cement rich mixture proportions and the use of smaller sized coarse aggregates, the compressive elastic modulus of KFRC is considerably lower than that of plain concrete with a similar compressive strength and this should be accounted for in design where the serviceability limit state is critical. Results from splitting tension tests and ﬂexural tests indicate that the ACI 318M-08 [27] provisions related to the tensile strength of conven- tional concrete are also appropriate for KFRC. Further, the results of the ﬂexural tests indicate that KFRC exhibits a ductile failure mode compared to conventional concrete with an average measured toughness approximately three times that of similar plain concrete control specimens. As a result, KFRC could be used in the produc- tion of impact resisting members. Moreover, the observed improvement of the cracking behavior enhances the durability of concrete at relatively low cost compared to other types of ﬁbers. Thus, KFRC could be used for casting slab on grade. Scanning elec- tron micrographs indicate the good bond between the kenaf ﬁbers and the surrounding matrix and provide some hints regarding the possible failure mode and damage progression of the KFRC which could facilitate future modeling efforts. The research ﬁndings of this study indicate that kenaf is a promising material for the man- ufacture of sustainable, ‘green’ concrete by CO 2 sequestration from the atmosphere and represents a good replacement crop for the declining tobacco industry. Unfortunately, the KFRC requires the use of a cement rich mixture compared to conventional concrete resulting in more CO 2 emissions during the cement manufacturing process. Future research should focus on the use of cementitious mineral admixtures to offset the increased cement demand, and in- clude a comprehensive life-cycle assessment. Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge the support of 3F LLC and the North Carolina Green Fund, Grant Number G30433 001008GBF, for sponsoring this research through the National Sci- ence Foundation (NSF) Industry/University Cooperative Research Center (I/UCRC) on Repair of Buildings and Bridges with Compos- ites (RB 2C) at the North Carolina State University Construction Facilities Laboratory.
